eras.name: Viking Age Dress
eras.description: Norse garments were functional yet richly symbolic, reflecting social rank through brooches, dyes, and imported fabrics. Wool and linen dominated, with elaborate tablet-woven bands and animal-motif metalwork showcasing skilled craftsmanship.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Viking-era Danish woman wearing a blue wool hangerok apron dress over a white linen underdress, bronze tortoise brooches at the shoulders with bead chains between them, standing on a windswept Jutland coastline, dramatic golden-hour lighting.

eras.name: Renaissance & Court Fashion
eras.description: Danish royalty adopted European court fashion with local adaptations, importing silks and velvets while maintaining robust wool traditions. The reign of Christian IV brought extravagant Baroque styles to Copenhagen's aristocracy.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Danish nobleman from the court of Christian IV wearing a richly slashed crimson velvet doublet with gold thread, a starched lace ruff collar, standing in Rosenborg Castle's Great Hall, warm candlelit atmosphere.

eras.name: Danish Golden Age & Biedermeier
eras.description: The Danish Golden Age in art and culture coincided with restrained Biedermeier fashion that emphasized modest elegance. Copenhagen's bourgeoisie favored clean silhouettes and quality materials over excessive ornamentation.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Danish Golden Age portrait of a Copenhagen woman in a high-waisted muslin dress with gigot sleeves, draped cashmere shawl, seated in a sunlit parlor with Vilhelm Hammershøi-style muted tones, soft diffused daylight.

eras.name: National Romantic Folk Revival
eras.description: Rising Danish nationalism sparked renewed interest in regional folk costumes, particularly the distinctive Amager dress with its elaborate embroidery. This period bridged rural traditions with urban Arts and Crafts sensibilities.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Danish woman in a traditional Amager folk costume with richly embroidered bodice, striped wool skirt, and white linen headdress, standing among half-timbered houses on a cobblestone village street, warm autumn light.

eras.name: Danish Modernism & Design
eras.description: The Danish Modern movement's emphasis on simplicity, function, and natural materials profoundly shaped local fashion aesthetics. Clean lines and honest materials became the DNA of Scandinavian style.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 1960s Danish woman in a clean-lined camel wool coat and black turtleneck, standing in a modernist interior with Arne Jacobsen Egg chairs and teak furniture, cool Nordic daylight streaming through large windows.

eras.name: Copenhagen Cool & Scandi Style
eras.description: Copenhagen Fashion Week became a global leader in sustainable fashion, mandating sustainability requirements for participating brands. Danish street style blends minimalism with playful colors, establishing a new paradigm for responsible fashion.
eras.aiImagePrompt: Copenhagen Fashion Week street style featuring a Danish woman in an oversized pastel blazer, wide-leg trousers, and chunky sneakers, cycling past colorful Nyhavn canal houses, bright overcast Scandinavian light, candid editorial shot.

textiles.name: Bornholm Wool
textiles.description: Hardy wool from the Baltic island of Bornholm, known for its durability and warmth, traditionally used for outerwear and blankets.
textiles.technique: Traditional loom weaving with natural lanolin-rich fleece

textiles.name: Hedebo Embroidery
textiles.description: A distinctive Danish whitework embroidery tradition featuring geometric cutwork and pulled-thread patterns on linen.
textiles.technique: Cutwork and drawn-thread whitework on fine linen

textiles.name: Amager Brocade
textiles.description: Richly patterned silk and wool brocades used in the distinctive folk costumes of Amager island, reflecting Dutch settler influences.
textiles.technique: Supplementary weft brocading with metallic and silk threads

textiles.name: Organic Danish Hemp
textiles.description: Revived heritage fiber now central to Denmark's sustainable fashion movement, grown without pesticides in Danish soil.
textiles.technique: Modern industrial weaving of organically cultivated hemp fiber

climate.fashionImplication: Denmark's cool, damp climate drives demand for functional layering, waterproof outerwear, and cozy hygge-inspired knitwear. The cycling culture of Copenhagen has uniquely shaped fashion toward practical yet stylish garments that work on two wheels.

culturalInfluences: Danish Modern design philosophy (form follows function)
Hans Christian Andersen and literary romanticism
